content.text: Launch communications and support preparation are the systematic planning and execution activities that ensure users, stakeholders, and support teams are informed, prepared, and ready when an AI feature or product ships. This is the human-facing complement to technical readiness: while engineering validates that the system works correctly under load, launch communications ensure that everyone who interacts with or is affected by the launch can do so successfully.

For AI and agentic systems specifically, this phase has heightened importance. Users often arrive with misconceptions about what the system can and cannot do — shaped by media coverage, prior AI experiences, and the general mystique around the technology. If launch communications do not proactively address capabilities, limitations, and expected behaviors, users will fill the gaps with expectations the system cannot meet, generating frustration and support volume that drowns out genuine adoption signals.

Launch communications span two distinct streams that must be coordinated: external communications (reaching users, customers, and the public) and internal communications (reaching support agents, sales, account management, executives, and partner teams). Both streams must be triggered in the right sequence, at the right time, with the right level of detail for each audience. Support preparation bridges these streams: it is the work that enables support agents to become effective frontline representatives of the new capability the moment it is live.

The first 48 hours after launch set the tone for adoption, perception, and incident response. A technically successful launch (zero downtime, no regressions) can still be perceived as a failure if users do not understand the feature, cannot find documentation, get inconsistent answers from support, and encounter messaging that contradicts what they see in the product. Conversely, a launch with minor technical issues can still succeed commercially if communications are clear, support is responsive, and users feel informed.

For AI products and agentic systems, the stakes are elevated for several specific reasons: (1) User expectations for AI are often miscalibrated in both directions — either too high (expecting perfect performance) or too low (dismissing the capability as unreliable). Communications must anchor expectations accurately. (2) AI system outputs are probabilistic and context-dependent in ways that differ from deterministic software — support teams need conceptual frameworks to explain this, not just feature documentation. (3) AI safety and privacy are active user concerns. Proactive communications about data usage, model training, and output reliability build trust that reactive support cannot recover once lost.

Inadequate launch communications create compounding costs: high support volume consumes engineering time for escalations; inaccurate public statements create PR risks; user frustration from unmet expectations drives churn; internal teams not properly briefed give inconsistent answers that erode trust. All of these are avoidable with systematic preparation done in the weeks before launch.

Think of launch communications as telling the same story in multiple languages to multiple audiences simultaneously, coordinating the translations, and making sure everyone hears their version at the right time. The core story (what we built, why it matters, what it does and does not do) is the same for all audiences. But the CMO needs a one-pager with business impact metrics; the support agent needs a troubleshooting decision tree; the user needs a clear email with a call to action; the executive needs confidence it will not blow up in the press.

The support preparation side follows a different mental model: the support team is a distributed early warning system. When something goes wrong — unexpectedly, immediately after launch — support agents are the first to hear about it through user tickets, chat, and social monitoring. A well-prepared support team can distinguish 'user confusion about the feature' from 'genuine technical defect' from 'edge case that engineering needs to evaluate' and escalate appropriately, enabling rapid response. Without preparation, all three get treated as bugs, flooding engineering with noise.

A useful framing for the entire launch prep sequence: work backwards from launch day and identify every question each stakeholder or user group will have in the first 72 hours, then ensure there is a clear, consistent, pre-approved answer for each before the launch button is pressed.

Proactive communications (clear in-app messaging, well-structured documentation, FAQ, email) answer the most common questions before users need to contact support. A well-executed launch typically reduces per-user support ticket rate by 30–60% compared to launches without preparation.

When sales, support, marketing, and the product itself all say the same thing about what the feature does, users receive a coherent experience. Without coordination, each team fills gaps with their own interpretation, creating contradictions that undermine trust.

A prepared support team with clear escalation paths, monitoring dashboards, and runbooks can detect and escalate genuine technical issues in minutes rather than hours. The difference between a 15-minute and a 2-hour time-to-escalation on a critical bug can determine whether an incident becomes a public outage report.

Users who understand what an AI feature can and cannot do are more forgiving of limitations and more likely to find genuine value. Users who expected something different churn quickly and leave negative reviews.

Executives, board members, and major customers who receive a structured launch brief before launch are prepared to speak confidently about the new capability. This is especially important for enterprise AI launches where buyers need to brief their own teams.

The process of writing support documentation and knowledge base articles forces the product team to articulate edge cases, limitations, and expected behaviors precisely — often surfacing gaps in product design that can be addressed before launch.

Thorough launch communications preparation takes 2–4 weeks of part-time effort from product, marketing, support, and engineering stakeholders. This competes with final pre-launch engineering work and can feel like overhead when the team is under pressure.
Pre-launch internal communications about confidential features create information security risks. Too wide an internal distribution before NDA controls are in place can leak launch details.

Every knowledge base article and user-facing document written for launch requires ongoing maintenance as the product evolves. A large launch documentation corpus can become a maintenance burden if not actively managed.

Sending too many communications to users before and after launch — especially in email — trains them to tune out, reducing the effectiveness of future critical communications (like service disruption notices).

Comprehensive documentation takes longer to produce and risks being inaccurate if the product changes in final pre-launch iterations. The discipline of keeping documentation updated in the final week before launch is operationally challenging.

The preparation phase begins with a launch communications brief — a single document that defines the target audience, key messages, channel strategy, timing sequence, success metrics, and ownership for each communication activity. This brief is the source of truth that prevents each team from developing their own independent messaging that contradicts others.

The brief documents the feature's capabilities and limitations in plain language — not marketing copy, not technical specifications, but clear statements of what the system will and will not do. These capability/limitation statements are shared with support, sales, and marketing so every customer-facing team is working from the same ground truth. For AI features, this explicitly includes accuracy characteristics ('the model is right approximately X% of the time on task Y, per our evaluation suite') and known failure modes.

Support preparation runs in parallel: reviewing existing knowledge base structure, drafting new articles for the new feature, updating agent training materials, revising escalation decision trees to include the new feature's specific failure modes, and briefing the support team on how the AI system works conceptually (not just procedurally).

In the final two weeks, communications content is finalized, reviewed, and approved. Internal stakeholders receive their briefings (often phased: executives first, then managers, then individual contributors). Beta or early-access communications go out to the relevant user segments. In-product messaging (tooltips, onboarding flows, banners) is implemented and QA'd. Status page and documentation updates are staged for deployment on launch day.

The support war room (or equivalent on-call rotation) is scheduled and briefed. Monitoring dashboards are configured to track real-time support volume, sentiment, and key error categories. An incident response runbook specific to the launch is circulated and read by the on-call team.

On launch day, communications are deployed in a specific sequence: (1) status page updated with the new feature's presence, (2) in-product messaging activated, (3) external launch email sent, (4) documentation published, (5) social media posts published, (6) press releases distributed (if applicable). This sequence ensures users can always find more information before they're asked to use the feature.

The support war room monitors in real time: ticket volume, first-response time, top ticket categories, social sentiment, and system health metrics. Pre-defined thresholds trigger escalation protocols (e.g., if ticket volume exceeds 2× baseline in the first hour, the incident commander escalates to engineering).

After the initial launch period, a retrospective captures what worked and what did not in the communications process. Metrics are reviewed: support volume vs. baseline, satisfaction scores, email open and click rates, documentation views, and user adoption indicators. Knowledge base articles are updated based on actual support ticket patterns — the gaps between what was documented and what users actually asked about. A post-launch user communication (at T+1 week) summarizes what users have accomplished and teases upcoming improvements.
The diagram below illustrates the timeline and channel mapping for a typical 4-week launch communications sequence, from stakeholder briefs to post-launch retrospective.

attrs.alt: Launch communications timeline: T-4 weeks to launch day and beyond, with internal/external phases, support prep, launch day activities, and channel priority mapping

content.text: The channel sequence on launch day is critical: status page and in-app messaging reach users immediately and can be updated without stakeholder approval cycles. Email reaches users who are not currently in the product. Documentation is the persistent, authoritative reference. Social media amplifies reach but should come last since corrections require public acknowledgment.
Any customer-facing feature launch, regardless of size — even 'small' features can generate outsized support volume if they change established user workflows.
AI feature launches — where user mental models are most likely to be miscalibrated and where limitations need explicit communication to prevent harmful misuse.
Launches affecting enterprise or regulated users — who have their own internal change management and training processes that require advance notice.
Features with pricing changes — where user communications have contractual or legal implications.
Breaking changes to existing workflows — where users who do not adapt will experience degraded functionality.
Release notes update, changelog email, KB article. 1-2 days of prep.
In-app announcement, blog post, updated docs, support team brief. 1 week of prep.
Major feature / product launch (>30% of users or new product):
Full communications plan: stakeholder briefs, phased external rollout, war room, press outreach, full documentation suite. 3-4 weeks of prep.

# Launch Communications Brief: [Feature Name]

## Overview
- **Feature**: [Name and one-sentence description]
- **Launch date**: [Date and time, timezone]
- **Launched to**: [All users / Beta segment / Enterprise tier / etc.]
- **Brief owner**: [Name, role]
- **Last updated**: [Date]

## Core Messages (approved, use verbatim in all comms)
1. **What it is**: [One sentence, plain language, no jargon]
2. **What it does for users**: [The core user benefit]
3. **Key limitations**: [What it does NOT do; critical for AI features]
4. **How to get started**: [Single clear action]

## Audience and Channel Matrix
| Audience         | Channel          | Timing        | Owner     |
|-----------------|-----------------|---------------|-----------|
| All customers    | Email           | Launch day 9am | Marketing |
| Active sessions  | In-app banner   | Launch day 9am | Product   |
| Enterprise customers | CSM 1:1 call | T-2 days     | CS team   |
| Internal team    | Slack #product  | T-3 days      | PM        |
| Executives       | Exec brief doc  | T-5 days      | Director  |
| Press            | Press release   | Embargo lift  | PR        |

## Capability / Limitation Statements (for support team)
**CAN do:**
- [Specific, testable statement of capability]
- [Include quantitative accuracy metrics where available]

**CANNOT do:**
- [Specific limitation; especially important for AI features]
- [Known edge cases that produce degraded output]

**Will NOT do (safety/policy):**
- [Refusal behaviors; critical for AI systems]

## Success Metrics
- D+1 adoption rate: [target]
- D+7 active usage: [target]
- Support ticket volume (first 48h): [threshold above which we escalate]
- CSAT from support interactions: [target]

## Escalation Path
1. Support agent cannot resolve → Support lead
2. Support lead identifies pattern → Product + Engineering Slack
3. Engineering confirms bug → Incident commander page
4. Incident commander assesses severity → CEO/VP alert if P0/P1

## Pre-Launch Checklist
- [ ] KB articles drafted and reviewed
- [ ] Support team briefed (date: __)
- [ ] Email drafted, reviewed, scheduled
- [ ] In-app messaging implemented and QA'd
- [ ] Documentation published (or staged)
- [ ] Monitoring dashboard configured
- [ ] War room schedule confirmed
- [ ] Escalation contacts confirmed available on launch day

# [Feature Name]: How It Works and Troubleshooting Guide

**Audience**: Support agents (internal)  
**Last reviewed**: [Date]  
**Related docs**: [Link to public docs] | [Link to runbook]

---

## Quick Summary for Support Agents
[Feature name] is an AI-powered [description]. Key things to know before taking tickets:
- It uses [brief description of AI mechanism] to [core function]
- Accuracy on [primary task]: approximately [X%] based on internal testing
- Known limitation: [most common expected failure mode]
- It WILL NOT [key safety refusal behavior]

## Common User Questions and Approved Responses

### "Why did the AI give me a wrong answer?"
**Response template**: "I'm sorry the response wasn't what you expected. [Feature name] is AI-powered and provides suggestions based on patterns in data. It works best for [ideal use case] and may produce less accurate results for [edge case]. You can [action to improve results]. Let me know if you'd like me to look at your specific case."

**Note for agents**: Do NOT say the AI is "broken" or "wrong" — frame as limitations of the current system. If the error is reproducible and appears systematic, escalate to [Escalation channel] with the exact prompt and output.

### "Is my data being used to train the AI?"
**Response template**: Refer to our [Privacy Policy link]. [Feature name] uses your inputs to generate responses during your session. [Specify any training data usage per your actual policy.]

**Note**: This is a sensitive question. Only provide factual information; do not speculate about future data practices.

### "The AI refused to help me with [X]. Why?"
**Response template**: "[Feature name] is designed to assist with [supported use cases] and has guidelines about certain types of content. If you believe your request should be supported and was incorrectly declined, please share the details and I will review with our team."

**Escalation note**: Log all refusal-related tickets with the exact prompt to track if safety filters are over-refusing on legitimate use cases.

---

## Troubleshooting Decision Tree

**User reports unexpected output:**
1. Is the output factually wrong on a verifiable fact?
   - Yes → Log as [Category: AI Output Quality], note the prompt and output
   - No → Proceed to 2
2. Is the output in the wrong format (e.g., supposed to be JSON but isn't)?
   - Yes → Check if user modified the system prompt; if not, log as [Category: Format Error]
3. Is the response refusing a legitimate request?
   - Yes → Log as [Category: Safety Filter], priority HIGH, tag for PM review
4. Is the output blank or the system erroring out?
   - Yes → Check [Status Page link] for known incidents; if no incident, log as [Category: Availability], escalate to [Engineering channel]

---

## Escalation Thresholds
- **Escalate immediately**: >3 tickets in 1 hour reporting identical issue → possible systematic bug
- **Escalate within 2h**: Any ticket suggesting data exposure, privacy violation, or harmful output
- **Batch for weekly review**: General accuracy complaints, UX confusion, feature requests
This script automates dispatching different communications to the correct channel based on audience segment and timing, with dry-run support for pre-launch validation:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Launch communications dispatcher.
Reads a launch config and dispatches communications to each channel at the configured time.
Supports dry-run mode for pre-launch validation.
"""
import json
import smtplib
import logging
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable, Optional
from email.mime.text import MIMEText
from email.mime.multipart import MIMEMultipart

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format="%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(message)s")
log = logging.getLogger(__name__)


@dataclass
class CommunicationTask:
    name: str
    audience: str
    channel: str              # "email" | "slack" | "inapp" | "statuspage"
    scheduled_at: datetime
    content: dict             # channel-specific payload
    dry_run: bool = True


@dataclass
class LaunchConfig:
    feature_name: str
    launch_at: datetime
    tasks: list[CommunicationTask] = field(default_factory=list)


def dispatch_email(task: CommunicationTask, smtp_config: dict) -> bool:
    """Send an email communication."""
    if task.dry_run:
        log.info(f"[DRY RUN] Would send email to {task.content['to']}: {task.content['subject']}")
        return True
    try:
        msg = MIMEMultipart("alternative")
        msg["Subject"] = task.content["subject"]
        msg["From"] = task.content["from"]
        msg["To"] = task.content["to"]
        msg.attach(MIMEText(task.content["body_text"], "plain"))
        if "body_html" in task.content:
            msg.attach(MIMEText(task.content["body_html"], "html"))
        with smtplib.SMTP(smtp_config["host"], smtp_config["port"]) as server:
            server.starttls()
            server.login(smtp_config["user"], smtp_config["password"])
            server.send_message(msg)
        log.info(f"Email sent: {task.name} to {task.content['to']}")
        return True
    except Exception as e:
        log.error(f"Email failed for {task.name}: {e}")
        return False


def dispatch_slack(task: CommunicationTask, webhook_url: str) -> bool:
    """Post to a Slack channel via webhook."""
    import urllib.request
    payload = {
        "text": task.content.get("text", ""),
        "blocks": task.content.get("blocks", []),
    }
    if task.dry_run:
        log.info(f"[DRY RUN] Would post to Slack: {payload['text'][:80]}")
        return True
    try:
        data = json.dumps(payload).encode()
        req = urllib.request.Request(webhook_url, data=data,
                                     headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"})
        urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=10)
        log.info(f"Slack posted: {task.name}")
        return True
    except Exception as e:
        log.error(f"Slack post failed for {task.name}: {e}")
        return False


def run_launch_sequence(config: LaunchConfig, dispatchers: dict[str, Callable]) -> dict:
    """Execute all communications tasks in time order, returning a results report."""
    results = {"success": [], "failed": [], "skipped": []}
    now = datetime.now(tz=timezone.utc)
    sorted_tasks = sorted(config.tasks, key=lambda t: t.scheduled_at)

    for task in sorted_tasks:
        if task.scheduled_at > now:
            log.info(f"Skipping future task: {task.name} (scheduled {task.scheduled_at})")
            results["skipped"].append(task.name)
            continue

        dispatch_fn = dispatchers.get(task.channel)
        if not dispatch_fn:
            log.warning(f"No dispatcher for channel '{task.channel}', skipping {task.name}")
            results["skipped"].append(task.name)
            continue

        success = dispatch_fn(task)
        if success:
            results["success"].append(task.name)
        else:
            results["failed"].append(task.name)

    return results


# ── Example usage ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
if __name__ == "__main__":
    launch_time = datetime(2024, 9, 15, 9, 0, 0, tzinfo=timezone.utc)

    config = LaunchConfig(
        feature_name="AI Assistant v2.0",
        launch_at=launch_time,
        tasks=[
            CommunicationTask(
                name="internal-slack-engineering",
                audience="Engineering team",
                channel="slack",
                scheduled_at=launch_time,
                content={
                    "text": ":rocket: AI Assistant v2.0 is LIVE. Monitoring dashboard: [link]. War room: #launch-war-room. Escalation: page @oncall.",
                },
                dry_run=True,
            ),
            CommunicationTask(
                name="user-launch-email",
                audience="All users",
                channel="email",
                scheduled_at=launch_time,
                content={
                    "from": "product@example.com",
                    "to": "users@example.com",
                    "subject": "Introducing AI Assistant v2.0 — smarter, faster, now available",
                    "body_text": "Hi there,\n\nAI Assistant v2.0 is now available in your account.\n\nWhat's new:\n- [Feature 1]\n- [Feature 2]\n\nWhat it doesn't do yet: [Honest limitations]\n\nGet started: [link]\n\nQuestions? [support link]",
                },
                dry_run=True,
            ),
        ],
    )

    dispatchers_map = {
        "email": lambda task: dispatch_email(task, smtp_config={}),
        "slack": lambda task: dispatch_slack(task, webhook_url="https://hooks.slack.com/..."),
    }

    results = run_launch_sequence(config, dispatchers_map)
    print("Launch sequence results:", json.dumps(results, indent=2))
For AI feature launches, classifying incoming support tickets by root cause allows support leads to quickly identify systematic issues and trigger escalations before problems compound:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Simple rule-based support ticket triage for AI feature launches.
In production, you may augment this with an LLM classifier.
"""
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
import re

class TicketCategory(Enum):
    AI_OUTPUT_QUALITY = "ai_output_quality"    # AI gave wrong or poor answer
    SAFETY_FILTER = "safety_filter"            # AI refused something it should not have
    AVAILABILITY = "availability"              # System unavailable or erroring
    BILLING = "billing"                        # Pricing/access questions
    UX_CONFUSION = "ux_confusion"              # User doesn't understand the UI
    FEATURE_REQUEST = "feature_request"        # Request for new capabilities
    DATA_PRIVACY = "data_privacy"              # Questions about data usage
    UNKNOWN = "unknown"

class Priority(Enum):
    P0 = 0   # Immediate escalation
    P1 = 1   # < 1 hour
    P2 = 2   # < 4 hours
    P3 = 3   # < 24 hours

@dataclass
class TriageResult:
    category: TicketCategory
    priority: Priority
    routing: str                              # team or person to route to
    suggested_response_template: str
    escalate_immediately: bool

TRIAGE_RULES = [
    (r"(data|privacy|store|train|train on|gdpr|data retention)",
     TicketCategory.DATA_PRIVACY, Priority.P1, "privacy-team",
     "data_privacy_template", True),
    (r"(harm|dangerous|weapon|illegal|threat|violence|hate)",
     TicketCategory.SAFETY_FILTER, Priority.P1, "trust-safety",
     "safety_escalation_template", True),
    (r"(down|unavailable|500|error|can't access|not working|outage)",
     TicketCategory.AVAILABILITY, Priority.P0, "engineering-oncall",
     "availability_template", True),
    (r"(wrong|incorrect|hallucinate|made up|false|inaccurate|bad answer)",
     TicketCategory.AI_OUTPUT_QUALITY, Priority.P2, "ai-quality-team",
     "quality_complaint_template", False),
    (r"(refused|won't|blocked|prevent|not allow|shouldn't have)",
     TicketCategory.SAFETY_FILTER, Priority.P2, "ai-quality-team",
     "false_refusal_template", False),
    (r"(charge|price|bill|cost|subscription|tier|upgrade)",
     TicketCategory.BILLING, Priority.P3, "billing-support",
     "billing_template", False),
    (r"(how do|where is|can't find|confused|don't understand|tutorial)",
     TicketCategory.UX_CONFUSION, Priority.P3, "support-general",
     "ux_help_template", False),
    (r"(would love|feature request|wish|add|please add|when will)",
     TicketCategory.FEATURE_REQUEST, Priority.P3, "product-inbox",
     "feature_request_template", False),
]

def triage_ticket(subject: str, body: str) -> TriageResult:
    """Classify a support ticket and determine routing and priority."""
    text = (subject + " " + body).lower()

    for pattern, category, priority, routing, template, escalate in TRIAGE_RULES:
        if re.search(pattern, text, re.IGNORECASE):
            return TriageResult(
                category=category,
                priority=priority,
                routing=routing,
                suggested_response_template=template,
                escalate_immediately=escalate,
            )

    return TriageResult(
        category=TicketCategory.UNKNOWN,
        priority=Priority.P3,
        routing="support-general",
        suggested_response_template="generic_template",
        escalate_immediately=False,
    )


# ── Example tickets ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
test_tickets = [
    ("AI gave me wrong information", "I asked about my account balance and it said $0 but I have money"),
    ("Can't access the assistant", "Getting 500 error every time I try to use the AI feature"),
    ("Data privacy question", "Is my conversation stored and used to train your model?"),
    ("Refused my request", "The AI refused to help me write a cover letter, said it was not allowed"),
]

for subject, body in test_tickets:
    result = triage_ticket(subject, body)
    escalation_flag = " ⚠️ ESCALATE NOW" if result.escalate_immediately else ""
    print(f"Ticket: {subject!r}")
    print(f"  Category: {result.category.value}")
    print(f"  Priority: {result.priority.name}{escalation_flag}")
    print(f"  Route to: {result.routing}")
    print(f"  Template: {result.suggested_response_template}")
    print()

OpenAI's launch of ChatGPT in November 2022 is the canonical case study in AI launch communications — both as a masterclass in some aspects and a cautionary tale in others. The technical launch achieved rapid global scale. But the communications gaps created lasting challenges:
The product itself served as the primary communication vehicle — the demo was so self-evident that users immediately understood what it could do. This is the ideal scenario, but rare.

Explicit documentation of limitations (hallucination rate, knowledge cutoff, inability to access the internet) was absent from launch communications. Users discovered these limitations through frustrating experiences rather than managed expectations. This drove a second wave of negative press coverage focused on AI 'lying' — framed as a scandal rather than a known, documented limitation.

The volume of inquiries about inappropriate outputs, inaccurate statements, and policy edge cases overwhelmed what was available. The absence of a public escalation path for serious concerns forced users to post on Twitter and Reddit, creating visibility for issues that proper support routing would have contained.

The more impressive the capability, the more critical it is to proactively document limitations. Users who discover limitations through negative experiences have a more visceral reaction than those who read about them upfront. Proactive honesty about AI system limitations is a trust-building move, not a competitive disadvantage.

A more operationally rigorous example: enterprise B2B SaaS companies launching AI features to paying customers typically follow a staged rollout with structured communications at each stage: (1) Alpha/internal test with no external comms, (2) Closed beta with NDA'd customers, detailed technical brief, dedicated support contact, and weekly feedback calls, (3) Open beta with public announcement, changelog, full knowledge base, and expanded support coverage, (4) GA with comprehensive launch sequence. Each stage narrows the communication audience while increasing the preparation quality.
Mistake 1 — Conflating marketing copy with user expectations

Marketing emphasizes the best-case scenario. User expectations, once set by marketing copy, are not automatically reset by product limitations encountered in use. If the launch announcement says 'AI that always finds the right answer,' users will be frustrated when it doesn't — not because the AI failed, but because communications set an unachievable expectation. Use accurate, specific, testable language in all user-facing communications about AI capabilities.
Mistake 2 — Briefing support after the launch email goes out

Support agents receiving tickets about a feature they have never heard of is a common and entirely avoidable failure. The internal brief must reach the support team with enough lead time for them to read it, ask questions, and practice handling edge cases — at minimum 48 hours before launch, ideally 1 week. The sequence must be: (1) support team briefed, (2) launch communications sent.
Mistake 3 — No monitoring dashboard for launch day

Launching without a real-time view of support ticket volume, ticket categories, system health metrics, and social sentiment makes it impossible to distinguish between 'normal launch day noise' and 'something is genuinely wrong.' Define clear thresholds before launch: if ticket volume is 3× baseline in the first 2 hours, escalate to engineering. If social sentiment drops below a threshold, trigger the communications response plan.

Sending only an email assumes users are checking their email on launch day. In-product announcements reach active users immediately. Status page updates reach users monitoring for service changes. Documentation is discoverable by users who search. A well-executed launch uses all relevant channels in the right sequence.
Mistake 5 — No post-launch knowledge base update

The knowledge base articles written before launch are based on anticipated questions. Actual user questions often differ significantly. A knowledge base review at T+1 week — comparing what was written to what was actually asked in tickets — is the highest-value documentation maintenance activity in the first month. Skipping it leaves the documentation increasingly misaligned with user needs.
Write the FAQ from real user questions:

Seed your FAQ from beta feedback, support ticket patterns from related features, and user research. An FAQ written entirely from the product team's perspective of what questions users should ask will miss the questions users actually ask.

Research on marketing communications consistently shows that more than three key messages reduces retention of all of them. Pick the three most important things users need to know about the feature and reinforce them across all channels.

Before launch day, agree explicitly on what 'done' means: all communications sent, documentation live, support briefed, no P0 incidents in first 4 hours. Without explicit criteria, launch day drags indefinitely.
Separate the announcement from the explanation:

Launch emails should drive to action (try the feature, read the docs) — not explain how the feature works in detail. Link to documentation for depth. Users who receive a 2,000-word email will not read it; a 200-word email with a strong CTA will drive higher engagement.

Before launch, draft the email and Slack message you will send if something goes wrong. Having this pre-written means you can respond in minutes rather than hours when an incident occurs. The message should acknowledge the issue, state impact, provide a timeline for next update, and link to the status page.

Write documentation as if the feature is already live, then publish it on launch day. Staging documentation prevents the situation where users are told 'more info coming soon' on the day they first encounter the feature.
Run a war room dry run:

The day before launch, run through the war room protocol with the actual team that will be on watch. Confirm everyone knows how to access the monitoring dashboard, who to escalate to, and what the escalation thresholds are.

The performance metrics for launch communications are primarily operational rather than computational. The key indicators are: time-to-first-response for support tickets (target: < 2 hours for the first 24 hours after launch), ticket deflection rate (what fraction of expected tickets were avoided by proactive communications), and communications reach (email open rate, in-product banner dismissal rate indicating content read).
Spike suggests communications gap or product bug
Set SLA ahead of launch; add capacity if needed
Low rate suggests unresolvable questions → escalate pattern
Low open rate: subject line problem or email fatigue
Zero views means users aren't finding or seeking docs
Monitor for issues not surfaced via support tickets

Scalability of support is the most common operational bottleneck for large launches. If expected user adoption is 10× current user base, support capacity must scale proportionally. Options: tier-1 chatbot deflection using knowledge base content, community-driven support (user forums), and async support channels that allow agents to batch similar issues. For AI feature launches specifically, building a support chatbot powered by the same AI being launched is effective — it demonstrates the capability while handling volume.
Manage ticket routing, SLA tracking, and agent workflows. Configure launch-specific views to filter tickets by feature tag and priority.

Segment-based launch email campaigns with deliverability tracking, A/B testing, and automation triggers. Prefer transactional email providers (SendGrid, Postmark) for time-sensitive launch announcements.
Public-facing real-time status updates. Configure component-level visibility so the new AI feature has its own status component from launch day.
Monitor social media sentiment, brand mentions, and competitor mentions in real time during launch window.
Configure a dedicated #launch channel with automated status page alerts, monitoring alerts, and a pinned runbook link. Use threads to keep discussion organized during fast-moving launch day events.
CMS and documentation (Notion, Confluence, ReadMe, GitBook):
Stage and publish documentation. ReadMe and GitBook are optimized for developer documentation; Notion/Confluence for internal knowledge base.
Beta: email to segment; GA: full launch
Features needing iteration before broad release
Never appropriate for user-facing AI features

The 'no-comms ship' row exists only to name the anti-pattern explicitly. Every user-facing AI feature launch requires at minimum: accurate documentation of capabilities and limitations, support team awareness, and a mechanism for users to report issues. The cost of these minimums is low; the cost of launching without them is consistently high.

Internal communications about upcoming launches are frequently the source of public information leaks — either through inadvertent screen sharing, forwarded Slack messages, or disgruntled employees. Classify pre-launch communications at the appropriate confidentiality level, restrict distribution to need-to-know, and ensure NDAs are in place for external beta participants.

Launch communications about AI capabilities have legal implications in regulated industries (healthcare, finance, legal). Statements like 'AI-powered diagnosis' or 'AI investment advisor' trigger regulatory requirements. All user-facing launch communications about AI features should be reviewed by legal counsel in regulated contexts. Even in less regulated industries, claims about AI accuracy or reliability can expose the company to liability if the claims are not substantiated.

The incident response plan must include pre-written, legally reviewed communications templates. During an actual incident, time pressure causes teams to write poorly worded statements that create more problems than they solve. The incident comms workflow should be: (1) draft from pre-written template, (2) legal review (synchronously if possible), (3) publish to status page, (4) send email notification. Never improvise incident communications without templates.

Launch communications must be accessible to users with disabilities: email must support screen readers (semantic HTML, alt text for images, sufficient color contrast), in-product messaging must not rely solely on color to convey status, and documentation must be keyboard-navigable. This is both a legal requirement in many jurisdictions (WCAG 2.1 AA is the standard) and an ethical obligation.

Launch communications for AI features must explicitly document limitations, not just capabilities. Users who discover limitations through bad experiences have more visceral reactions than users who read about them upfront. Proactive honesty builds trust.

The internal communications sequence must precede the external communications sequence. The order is: support team briefed → key internal stakeholders briefed → beta/early-access users → general public. Reversing any part of this sequence creates operational chaos.

The most important metric for launch communications success is support ticket volume in the first 48 hours relative to baseline. Above 2× baseline signals either a communications gap (users confused) or a technical issue (bugs generating tickets). Pre-define this threshold and the escalation response before launch.

Pre-write the incident communications templates before launch day. An incident that occurs during a launch is the worst time to draft communications under pressure. Having approved templates enables a 15-minute response time vs. a 2-hour response time.

Documentation must be live before or simultaneously with the announcement. Users who click 'learn more' and reach a 404 or 'coming soon' page lose confidence in the product. Staging documentation for simultaneous launch is a hard operational requirement.

Knowledge base articles written before launch will not match the actual questions users ask. Schedule a T+1 week review to compare KB content to actual ticket patterns and update accordingly. This single activity closes more support gaps than any amount of pre-launch speculation.

For AI systems, the support team needs conceptual understanding (how the AI works, why it sometimes fails) not just procedural training (click here, then here). Agents who understand why the AI hallucinates can explain it credibly; agents with only a FAQ cannot handle the follow-up questions.

All launch communications should go through a single source-of-truth messaging brief that all teams reference. When marketing, sales, support, and the product UI all say slightly different things about what the AI does, users receive a fragmented, confusing experience. One brief, one message, many formats.
